item.title: Recovery scammers hit you when you’re down: Here’s how to avoid a second strike
item.description: If you’ve been the victim of fraud, you’re likely already a lead on a ‘sucker list’ – and if you’re not careful, your ordeal may be about to get worse.

item.title: As breakout time accelerates, prevention-first cybersecurity takes center stage
item.description: Threat actors are using AI to supercharge tried-and-tested TTPs. When attacks move this fast, cyber-defenders need to rethink their own strategy.

item.title: Digital assets after death: Managing risks to your loved one’s digital estate
item.description: Fraudsters often target the accounts of the deceased or their grieving relatives. Here’s how to keep the scammers at bay.

item.title: This month in security with Tony Anscombe – March 2026 edition
item.description: The past four weeks have seen a slew of new cybersecurity wake-up calls that showed why every organization needs a well-thought-out cyber-resilience plan

item.title: RSAC 2026 wrap-up – Week in security with Tony Anscombe
item.description: This year, AI agents took the center stage – as a defensive capability, but more pressingly as a risk many organizations haven't caught up with

item.title: A cunning predator: How Silver Fox preys on Japanese firms this tax season
item.description: Silver Fox is back in Japan, spoofing tax and HR emails timed to the one season when no one thinks twice about opening them

item.title: Virtual machines, virtually everywhere – and with real security gaps
item.description: Cloud VMs offer unmatched speed, scale and flexibility – all of which could eventually count for little if they’re left to fend for themselves

item.title: Cloud workload security: Mind the gaps
item.description: As IT infrastructure expands, visibility and control often lag behind – until an incident forces a reckoning

item.title: Move fast and save things: A quick guide to recovering a hacked account
item.description: What you do – and how fast – after an account is compromised often matters more than it may seem

item.title: EDR killers explained: Beyond the drivers
item.description: ESET researchers dive deeper into the EDR killer ecosystem, disclosing how attackers abuse vulnerable drivers

item.title: Face value: What it takes to fool facial recognition
item.description: ESET’s Jake Moore used smart glasses, deepfakes and face swaps to ‘hack’ widely-used facial recognition systems – and he'll demo it all at RSAC 2026

item.title: Cyber fallout from the Iran war: What to have on your radar
item.description: The cybersecurity implications of the war in the Middle East extend far beyond the region. Here’s where to focus your defenses.

item.title: Sednit reloaded: Back in the trenches
item.description: The resurgence of one of Russia’s most notorious APT groups

item.title: What cybersecurity actually does for your business
item.description: The ability to continue operating safely in an unsafe environment where competitors cannot is a competitive advantage that is rarely measured or discussed

item.title: How SMBs use threat research and MDR to build a defensive edge
item.description: We speak to Director of ESET Threat Research Jean-Ian Boutin about where solutions that blend advanced technology with human expertise provide the most practical value for businesses

item.title: Protecting education: How MDR can tip the balance in favor of schools
item.description: The education sector is notoriously short on cash, but rich in assets for threat actors to target. How can managed detection and response (MDR) help learning institutions regain the initiative?

item.title: This month in security with Tony Anscombe – February 2026 edition
item.description: In this roundup, Tony looks at how opportunistic threat actors are taking advantage of weak authentication, unmanaged exposure, and popular AI tools

item.title: Mobile app permissions (still) matter more than you may think
item.description: Start using a new app and you’ll often be asked to grant it permissions. But blindly accepting them could expose you to serious privacy and security risks.

item.title: Faking it on the phone: How to tell if a voice call is AI or not
item.description: Can you believe your ears? Increasingly, the answer is no. Here’s what’s at stake for your business, and how to beat the deepfakers.

item.title: PromptSpy ushers in the era of Android threats using GenAI
item.description: ESET researchers discover PromptSpy, the first known Android malware to abuse generative AI in its execution flow

item.title: Is Poshmark safe? How to buy and sell without getting scammed
item.description: Like any other marketplace, the social commerce platform has its share of red flags. It pays to know what to look for so you can shop or sell without headaches.

item.title: Is it OK to let your children post selfies online?
item.description: When it comes to our children’s digital lives, prohibition rarely works. It’s our responsibility to help them build a healthy relationship with tech.

item.title: Naming and shaming: How ransomware groups tighten the screws on victims
item.description: When corporate data is exposed on a dedicated leak site, the consequences linger long after the attack fades from the news cycle

item.title: Taxing times: Top IRS scams to look out for in 2026
item.description: It’s time to file your tax return. And cybercriminals are lurking to make an already stressful period even more edgy.

item.title: OfferUp scammers are out in force: Here’s what you should know
item.description: The mobile marketplace app has a growing number of users, but not all of them are genuine. Watch out for these common scams.

item.title: A slippery slope: Beware of Winter Olympics scams and other cyberthreats
item.description: It’s snow joke – sporting events are a big draw for cybercriminals. Make sure you’re not on the losing side by following these best practices.

item.title: This month in security with Tony Anscombe – January 2026 edition
item.description: The trends from January offer useful clues about the risks and priorities that security teams are likely to contend with throughout the year

item.title: DynoWiper update: Technical analysis and attribution
item.description: ESET researchers present technical details on a recent data destruction incident affecting a company in Poland’s energy sector

item.title: Love? Actually: Fake dating app used as lure in targeted spyware campaign in Pakistan
item.description: ESET researchers discover an Android spyware campaign targeting users in Pakistan via romance scam tactics, revealing links to a broader spy operation

item.title: Drowning in spam or scam emails? Here’s probably why
item.description: Has your inbox recently been deluged with unwanted and even outright malicious messages? Here are 10 possible reasons – and how to stem the tide.

item.title: ESET Research: Sandworm behind cyberattack on Poland’s power grid in late 2025
item.description: The attack involved data-wiping malware that ESET researchers have now analyzed and named DynoWiper

item.title: Children and chatbots: What parents should know
item.description: As children turn to AI chatbots for answers, advice, and companionship, questions emerge about their safety, privacy, and emotional development

item.title: Common Apple Pay scams, and how to stay safe
item.description: Here’s how the most common scams targeting Apple Pay users work and what you can do to stay one step ahead

item.title: Old habits die hard: 2025’s most common passwords were as predictable as ever
item.description: Once again, data shows an uncomfortable truth: the habit of choosing eminently hackable passwords is alive and well

item.title: Why LinkedIn is a hunting ground for threat actors – and how to protect yourself
item.description: The business social networking site is a vast, publicly accessible database of corporate information. Don’t believe everyone on the site is who they say they are.

item.title: Is it time for internet services to adopt identity verification?
item.description: Should verified identities become the standard online? Australia’s social media ban for under-16s shows why the question matters.

item.title: Your personal information is on the dark web. What happens next?
item.description: If your data is on the dark web, it’s probably only a matter of time before it’s abused for fraud or account hijacking. Here’s what to do.

item.title: Credential stuffing: What it is and how to protect yourself
item.description: Reusing passwords may feel like a harmless shortcut – until a single breach opens the door to multiple accounts

item.title: This month in security with Tony Anscombe – December 2025 edition
item.description: As 2025 draws to a close, Tony looks back at the cybersecurity stories that stood out both in December and across the whole of this year

item.title: A brush with online fraud: What are brushing scams and how do I stay safe?
item.description: Have you ever received a package you never ordered? It could be a warning sign that your data has been compromised, with more fraud to follow.

item.title: Revisiting CVE-2025-50165: A critical flaw in Windows Imaging Component
item.description: A comprehensive analysis and assessment of a critical severity vulnerability with low likelihood of mass exploitation

item.title: LongNosedGoblin tries to sniff out governmental affairs in Southeast Asia and Japan
item.description: ESET researchers discovered a China-aligned APT group, LongNosedGoblin, which uses Group Policy to deploy cyberespionage tools across networks of governmental institutions

item.title: ESET Threat Report H2 2025
item.description: A view of the H2 2025 threat landscape as seen by ESET telemetry and from the perspective of ESET threat detection and research experts

item.title: Black Hat Europe 2025: Was that device designed to be on the internet at all?
item.description: Behind the polished exterior of many modern buildings sit outdated systems with vulnerabilities waiting to be found

item.title: Black Hat Europe 2025: Reputation matters – even in the ransomware economy
item.description: Being seen as reliable is good for ‘business’ and ransomware groups care about 'brand reputation' just as much as their victims

item.title: Locks, SOCs and a cat in a box: What Schrödinger can teach us about cybersecurity
item.description: If you don’t look inside your environment, you can’t know its true state – and attackers count on that

item.title: Seeking symmetry during ATT&CK® season: How to harness today’s diverse analyst and tester landscape to paint a security masterpiece
item.description: Interpreting the vast cybersecurity vendor landscape through the lens of industry analysts and testing authorities can immensely enhance your cyber-resilience.

item.title: The big catch: How whaling attacks target top executives
item.description: Is your organization’s senior leadership vulnerable to a cyber-harpooning? Learn how to keep them safe.

item.title: Phishing, privileges and passwords: Why identity is critical to improving cybersecurity posture
item.description: Identity is effectively the new network boundary. It must be protected at all costs.

item.title: MuddyWater: Snakes by the riverbank
item.description: MuddyWater targets critical infrastructure in Israel and Egypt, relying on custom malware, improved tactics, and a predictable playbook

item.title: Oversharing is not caring: What’s at stake if your employees post too much online
item.description: From LinkedIn to X, GitHub to Instagram, there are plenty of opportunities to share work-related information. But posting could also get your company into trouble.

item.title: This month in security with Tony Anscombe – November 2025 edition
item.description: Data exposure by top AI companies, the Akira ransomware haul, Operation Endgame against major malware families, and more of this month's cybersecurity news

item.title: What parents should know to protect their children from doxxing
item.description: Online disagreements among young people can easily spiral out of control. Parents need to understand what’s at stake.

item.title: Influencers in the crosshairs: How cybercriminals are targeting content creators
item.description: Social media influencers can provide reach and trust for scams and malware distribution. Robust account protection is key to stopping the fraudsters.

item.title: MDR is the answer – now, what’s the question?
item.description: Why your business needs the best-of-breed combination of technology and human expertise

item.title: The OSINT advantage: Find your weak spots before attackers do
item.description: Here’s how open-source intelligence helps trace your digital footprint and uncover your weak points, plus a few essential tools to connect the dots

item.title: PlushDaemon compromises network devices for adversary-in-the-middle attacks
item.description: ESET researchers have discovered a network implant used by the China-aligned PlushDaemon APT group to perform adversary-in-the-middle attacks

item.title: What if your romantic AI chatbot can’t keep a secret?
item.description: Does your chatbot know too much? Here's why you should think twice before you tell your AI companion everything.

item.title: How password managers can be hacked – and how to stay safe
item.description: Look no further to learn how cybercriminals could try to crack your vault and how you can keep your logins safe

item.title: Why shadow AI could be your biggest security blind spot
item.description: From unintentional data leakage to buggy code, here’s why you should care about unsanctioned AI use in your company

item.title: In memoriam: David Harley
item.description: Former colleagues and friends remember the cybersecurity researcher, author, and mentor whose work bridged the human and technical sides of security

item.title: The who, where, and how of APT attacks in Q2 2025–Q3 2025
item.description: ESET Chief Security Evangelist Tony Anscombe highlights some of the key findings from the latest issue of the ESET APT Activity Report

item.title: ESET APT Activity Report Q2 2025–Q3 2025
item.description: An overview of the activities of selected APT groups investigated and analyzed by ESET Research in Q2 2025 and Q3 2025

item.title: Sharing is scaring: The WhatsApp scam you didn’t see coming
item.description: How a fast-growing scam is tricking WhatsApp users into revealing their most sensitive financial and other data

item.title: How social engineering works | Unlocked 403 cybersecurity podcast (S2E6)
item.description: Think you could never fall for an online scam? Think again. Here's how scammers could exploit psychology to deceive you – and what you can do to stay one step ahead

item.title: Ground zero: 5 things to do after discovering a cyberattack
item.description: When every minute counts, preparation and precision can mean the difference between disruption and disaster

item.title: This month in security with Tony Anscombe – October 2025 edition
item.description: From the end of Windows 10 support to scams on TikTok and state-aligned hackers wielding AI, October's headlines offer a glimpse of what's shaping cybersecurity right now

item.title: Fraud prevention: How to help older family members avoid scams
item.description: Families that combine open communication with effective behavioral and technical safeguards can cut the risk dramatically

item.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Month 2025: When seeing isn't believing
item.description: Deepfakes are blurring the line between real and fake and fraudsters are cashing in, using synthetic media for all manner of scams

item.title: Recruitment red flags: Can you spot a spy posing as a job seeker?
item.description: Here’s what to know about a recent spin on an insider threat – fake North Korean IT workers infiltrating western firms

item.title: How MDR can give MSPs the edge in a competitive market
item.description: With cybersecurity talent in short supply and threats evolving fast, managed detection and response is emerging as a strategic necessity for MSPs

item.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Month 2025: Cyber-risk thrives in the shadows
item.description: Shadow IT leaves organizations exposed to cyberattacks and raises the risk of data loss and compliance failures

item.title: Gotta fly: Lazarus targets the UAV sector
item.description: ESET research analyzes a recent instance of the Operation DreamJob cyberespionage campaign conducted by Lazarus, a North Korea-aligned APT group

item.title: SnakeStealer: How it preys on personal data – and how you can protect yourself
item.description: Here’s what to know about the malware with an insatiable appetite for valuable data, so much so that it tops this year's infostealer detection charts

item.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Month 2025: Building resilience against ransomware
item.description: Ransomware rages on and no organization is too small to be targeted by cyber-extortionists. How can your business protect itself against the threat?

item.title: Minecraft mods: Should you 'hack' your game?
item.description: Some Minecraft mods don’t help build worlds – they break them. Here’s how malware can masquerade as a Minecraft mod.

item.title: IT service desks: The security blind spot that may put your business at risk
item.description: Could a simple call to the helpdesk enable threat actors to bypass your security controls? Here’s how your team can close a growing security gap.

item.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Month 2025: Why software patching matters more than ever
item.description: As the number of software vulnerabilities continues to increase, delaying or skipping security updates could cost your business dearly.

item.title: AI-aided malvertising: Exploiting a chatbot to spread scams
item.description: Cybercriminals have tricked X’s AI chatbot into promoting phishing scams in a technique that has been nicknamed “Grokking”. Here’s what to know about it.

item.title: How Uber seems to know where you are – even with restricted location permissions
item.description: Is the ride-hailing app secretly tracking you? Not really, but this iOS feature may make it feel that way.

item.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Month 2025: Passwords alone are not enough
item.description: Never rely on just a password, however strong it may be. Multi-factor authentication is essential for anyone who wants to protect their online accounts from intruders.

item.title: The case for cybersecurity: Why successful businesses are built on protection
item.description: Company leaders need to recognize the gravity of cyber risk, turn awareness into action, and put security front and center

item.title: Beware of threats lurking in booby-trapped PDF files
item.description: Looks can be deceiving, so much so that the familiar icon could mask malware designed to steal your data and money.

item.title: Manufacturing under fire: Strengthening cyber-defenses amid surging threats
item.description: Manufacturers operate in one of the most unforgiving threat environments and face a unique set of pressures that make attacks particularly damaging

item.title: New spyware campaigns target privacy-conscious Android users in the UAE
item.description: ESET researchers have discovered campaigns distributing spyware disguised as Android Signal and ToTok apps, targeting users in the United Arab Emirates

item.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Month 2025: Knowledge is power
item.description: We're kicking off the month with a focus on the human element: the first line of defense, but also the path of least resistance for many cybercriminals

item.title: This month in security with Tony Anscombe – September 2025 edition
item.description: The past 30 days have seen no shortage of new threats and incidents that brought into sharp relief the need for well-thought-out cyber-resilience plans

item.title: Roblox executors: It’s all fun and games until someone gets hacked
item.description: You could be getting more than you bargained for when you download that cheat tool promising quick wins

item.title: DeceptiveDevelopment: From primitive crypto theft to sophisticated AI-based deception
item.description: Malware operators collaborate with covert North Korean IT workers, posing a threat to both headhunters and job seekers

item.title: Watch out for SVG files booby-trapped with malware
item.description: What you see is not always what you get as cybercriminals increasingly weaponize SVG files as delivery vectors for stealthy malware

item.title: Gamaredon X Turla collab
item.description: Notorious APT group Turla collaborates with Gamaredon, both FSB-associated groups, to compromise high‑profile targets in Ukraine

item.title: Small businesses, big targets: Protecting your business against ransomware
item.description: Long known to be a sweet spot for cybercriminals, small businesses are more likely to be victimized by ransomware than large enterprises

item.title: HybridPetya: The Petya/NotPetya copycat comes with a twist
item.description: HybridPetya is the fourth publicly known real or proof-of-concept bootkit with UEFI Secure Boot bypass functionality

item.title: Introducing HybridPetya: Petya/NotPetya copycat with UEFI Secure Boot bypass
item.description: UEFI copycat of Petya/NotPetya exploiting CVE-2024-7344 discovered on VirusTotal

item.title: Are cybercriminals hacking your systems – or just logging in?
item.description: As bad actors often simply waltz through companies’ digital front doors with a key, here’s how to keep your own door locked tight

item.title: Preventing business disruption and building cyber-resilience with MDR
item.description: Given the serious financial and reputational risks of incidents that grind business to a halt, organizations need to prioritize a prevention-first cybersecurity strategy

item.title: Under lock and key: Safeguarding business data with encryption
item.description: As the attack surface expands and the threat landscape grows more complex, it’s time to consider whether your data protection strategy is fit for purpose

item.title: GhostRedirector poisons Windows servers: Backdoors with a side of Potatoes
item.description: ESET researchers have identified a new threat actor targeting Windows servers with a passive C++ backdoor and a malicious IIS module that manipulates Google search results

item.title: This month in security with Tony Anscombe – August 2025 edition
item.description: From Meta shutting down millions of WhatsApp accounts linked to scam centers all the way to attacks at water facilities in Europe, August 2025 saw no shortage of impactful cybersecurity news

item.title: Don’t let “back to school” become “back to (cyber)bullying”
item.description: Cyberbullying is a fact of life in our digital-centric society, but there are ways to push back
